text: The surgeon performs an ambulatory laparoscopic myomectomy for a nine-centimeter myoma. Initially, the peritoneum is opened between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament to access the retroperitoneal space. The ureter is identified medially, and the space lateral to it is developed until the internal iliac artery is visible. The pararectal fossa is opened between the ureter and the internal iliac artery. The utero-umbilical tract is located, and the uterine artery is identified and clipped at its origin after medializing the left ureter. The same procedure is repeated on the right side, with medial traction applied to the IP ligament. The peritoneum is opened, revealing the ureteral rotation, and the avascular retroperitoneal space is developed. The uterine artery is tracked and clipped for vascular control of the uterus.

The surgeon performs a longitudinal incision above the posterior relief of the nine-centimeter type II-V myoma using a harmonic ACE device, cutting through the uterine serosa to reach the myometrial capsule. The procedure remains bloodless due to prior vascular control. A ten-millimeter forceps is used through the left iliac trocar to grasp and extract the myoma, cutting surrounding myometrial tissue to progressively remove the large myoma from its bed.

The surgeon applies ultrasonic energy directly to the myoma, avoiding the healthy myometrium to minimize post-operative complications. The myomectomy progresses smoothly upon finding the correct dissecting plane, eliminating the need for electrical hemostasis. Classical stitches are used to close the myoma bed, with a fourteen millimeter Vigrin One stitch placed three centimeters outside the uterine scar, ensuring quick and safe closure with excellent hemostasis.

The surgeon tracks the stitch and invaginates the healthy myometrium into the myoma bed, closing it using an intra-corporeal knotting technique. The first knot achieves over eighty percent hemostasis. The procedure is repeated upward, three centimeters outside the uterine scar, with a large bite of myometrial tissue, ensuring the proximal part of the tube is not included in the stitch. Traction on both stitches shows the healthy myometrium fully invaginated into the myoma bed, reducing the risk of post-operative complications. The closure continues with four to five stitches for the nine centimeter myoma.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal knotting to achieve a perfect closure of the uterine incision, demonstrating a well-reconstructed anatomical structure. The final knot results in blanching near the uterine scar, indicating effective hemostasis, while carefully avoiding damage to the nearby fallopian tube.

The surgeon completes the uterine reconstruction and begins morcellation using a fifteen millimeter morcellator positioned in the left iliac fossa, operating at seven hundred revolutions per minute. The operative trocar is closed to reduce the risk of incisional hernia, and the pelvis is cleaned thoroughly, marking the final aspect of the surgery.
